explanation: Bulk or isthmus fracture of amalgam is most often from inadequate preparation (insufficient depth/width, sharp internal angles, or weak isthmus), not diet investigation or veneer choices.
From birth to 6 months, fluoride supplement dose is 0 mg/day even in nonfluoridated areas; supplementation tables start only after 6 months of age based on water F level.
MI one year prior (stable) and resolved hepatitis B do not mandate prolonged antibiotics for routine RCT; short-course NSAID analgesia (naproxen ~5 days) is appropriate for post-endo pain control.

Reverse smoking produces thermal/chemical irritation of the palate yielding a reactive keratotic/stomatitis-type white change, not primarily a malignant or purely ‘benign neoplasm’ label on first presentation.
Fibromyalgia has no single pathognomonic lab or biopsy; diagnosis is clinical and by exclusion of other rheumatic, neurologic, and endocrine causes of widespread pain.

New severe unilateral facial/jaw pain with systemic features (anorexia) in a patient with polymyalgia rheumatica spectrum raises giant cell arteritis until proven otherwise—urgent, not simple myalgia.
Chlorhexidine’s clinical effectiveness rests largely on substantivity—binding to oral surfaces and releasing over hours—rather than tissue dissolution or nonspecific cytotoxicity.

Among external resorption types, surface (repair) resorption is the shallow, self-limiting cemental loss often shown as minimal external contour change; inflammatory and replacement types have more aggressive radiographic patterns.
A child who cannot sustain attention, is constantly handling instruments, and loses focus fits attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder behaviorally among the coherent options given.
Immediate palpitations after lidocaine (especially with epinephrine) most often mean inadvertent intravascular injection delivering a catecholamine bolus, not full systemic toxicity alone.

Combination syndrome pattern (upper CD + lower anterior teeth/RPD) yields flabby anterior maxillary ridge with epulis fissuratum and lower anterior overeruption/attrition when the upper prosthesis is removed.
A painful submandibular swelling with a firm palpable mass and a radiopaque ductal body is sialolithiasis (stone), not non-inflammatory sialadenosis or a simple duct cyst.
Planning a single anterior implant (#11) requires prosthetically driven workup: diagnostic casts/wax-up and a surgical template/guide—more essential than closed-tray technique alone.
Pain on release of occlusal pressure (rebound) is classic for a cracked tooth; vertical root fracture usually presents with deep isolated pockets and J-shaped bone loss after endo.
Buccal gingival swelling over a primary molar indicates abscess/acute infection and is a stronger definitive indication for extraction than isolated percussion pain alone.

When a substandard RCT is sealed under a well-fitting cast post–core and crown, orthograde retreatment is difficult; apical surgery with root-end preparation and retrofill is the usual definitive path.

Among psychiatric contexts, eating disorders (bulimia/purging) are classically linked to bilateral salivary gland enlargement/swelling; schizophrenia and bipolar are not primary causes of submandibular swellings.
Uncontrolled diabetes with acute facial swelling and severe trismus risks deep space infection and airway compromise; immediate hospital admission with CT and surgical/medical management is required.
Systemic corticosteroids raise blood glucose and can worsen glycemic control; continued steroid use in a diabetic predictably increases HbA1c despite prior good control on metformin.

With expired coverage, you may treat if the patient agrees to private/out-of-pocket payment; you should not bill as if insurance will retroactively cover or delay care on a false insurance promise alone.

Multiple punched-out radiolucencies of varying size in an adult skull/jaws are classic for multiple myeloma (or metastatic disease); Gorlin is typically fewer odontogenic keratocysts in a younger patient.

Sequence for deep caries possibly needing crown lengthening: excavate to assess restorability, take bitewings to evaluate remaining tooth structure/ferrule needs, complete RCT if indicated, then surgical crown lengthening.
Sodium hypochlorite irrigant is strongly alkaline (pH ~11–12), higher than chlorhexidine (~5.5–7), normal saline (~7), or phosphoric acid (very low pH).
The predominant inorganic component of dentin is hydroxyapatite (calcium phosphate mineral); collagen types are the organic matrix, not inorganic.

Diffuse erythematous gingivitis with oral mucosal ulcers in a viral primary herpetic pattern is managed with antivirals plus supportive care; antibiotics alone do not treat HSV and steroids can worsen viral disease.
Flumazenil is the competitive benzodiazepine receptor antagonist used to reverse midazolam sedation or overdose.
In preventive resin restorations, flowable composite wets and fills narrow pits/fissures better because of lower viscosity and lower filler load than packable materials.
In a young uncooperative child, fluoride varnish painted/wiped on is preferred over tray gels (less gag, less ingestion risk, better behavior tolerance) for professional topical fluoride.
‘Organic occlusion’ in classic occlusion teaching is mutually protected occlusion: posterior teeth protect anteriors in MIP, anteriors disclude posteriors in excursions.
A positive Paul–Bunnell (heterophile antibody) test supports infectious mononucleosis from EBV; it is not used to diagnose HSV, HPV, or SLE.
